subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 12! In this unit you will learn one of the most useful skills in all of everyday life: how to tell the time. Time is happening all around you, every single day. When Amina wakes up for Fajr prayer, when Yusuf leaves for school, when Halima's family gathers to break their fast at iftar during Ramadan, and when Omar closes his shop in the Hargeisa market in the evening – all of these are moments marked by the clock. When you can read a clock, you understand the shape of your whole day.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured directly from the standardized Cambridge Stage 2 workbook source rows for screen presentation.

outcomes: This unit is designed for self-paced learning. That means you move at your own speed. Read each explanation slowly, look carefully at the clock pictures described in words, and practise on a real clock whenever you can. Your AI tutor is available day and night to give you extra practice, to describe a clock face in a new way, or to check your answers. Your teacher will also meet with you about twice a week to help with anything that feels tricky.

Why does telling the time matter so much? Time helps us organise our day and know when to do things. Just as we use a ruler to measure length and a scale to measure weight, we use a clock to measure time. Traders know when the market opens. Families know when to gather for prayer, because the five daily prayers each come at their own time. Bus drivers between Mogadishu and Kismaayo follow a timetable. When you become confident at telling the time, you gain a skill you will use for the rest of your life – and you become more independent, because you can plan and organise yourself.

As you work, it will help enormously to keep a real clock beside you – a wall clock, a watch, or a toy clock you can turn with your hands. Mathematics is not a subject you can learn only by reading; you must do it. Move the hands, say the time out loud, and picture the clock in your mind. If you read a time wrongly, that is not a failure; it simply shows exactly what to practise next. Every person who reads a clock easily once had to learn these very same ideas, one careful step at a time.
Name the two main kinds of clock: the analog clock and the digital clock.
Point to the parts of an analog clock: the face, the numbers, the hour hand and the minute hand.
Read times to the o'clock (for example 3 o'clock).
Read times to the half past (for example half past 4).
Read times to the quarter past and quarter to the hour.
Count in fives around the clock to work out the minutes.
Match an analog time to the same digital time (for example half past 2 and 2:30).

concepts.title: Two Kinds of Clock
concepts.explanation: There are two main kinds of clock that we see every day, and they both do the same job: they tell us what time it is. The first is the analog clock. It is round, it has the numbers 1 to 12 arranged in a circle, and it has hands that point to the numbers. The big wall clock in the masjid is usually an analog clock. The second is the digital clock. It shows the time using numbers with a small dot in the middle, like 7:30. The clock on a phone, a microwave, or a car dashboard is usually a digital clock. Both clocks…
concepts.example: In this unit we will spend most of our time on the analog clock, because once you understand the hands going round, the digital clock becomes very easy. Think of the analog clock as the one that helps you truly *see*…

concepts.title: The Parts of an Analog Clock
concepts.explanation: Before we can read an analog clock, we must know its parts, just as you must know the parts of your body before you can describe them. Picture a round clock in front of you. Here are its parts: The face is the round part. It holds everything else.
concepts.example: The numbers go from 1 to 12 all the way around the edge, like the numbers on a plate arranged in a ring. The 12 sits at the very top, the 6 sits at the very bottom, the 3 is on the right, and the 9 is on the left.

concepts.title: Reading O'clock Times
concepts.explanation: The easiest time to read is an o'clock time. A time is 'o'clock' when the minute hand points straight up to the 12, and the hour hand points exactly at a number. When this happens, we say the time is that number o'clock. If the hour hand points to the 3 and the minute hand points to the 12, the time is 3 o'clock. If the hour hand points to the 8 and the minute hand points to the 12, the time is 8 o'clock. The word 'o'clock' is a short way of saying 'of the clock'. It tells us that the time is a whole hour, with no…
concepts.example: Let us read a few together. Imagine the hour hand on the 7 and the minute hand on the 12: that is 7 o'clock, the time Amina might leave for school. Imagine the hour hand on the 12 and the minute hand also on the 12:…

concepts.title: Half Past the Hour
concepts.explanation: After o'clock times, the next easiest time to read is half past. 'Half past' means the minute hand has gone halfway around the clock. When the minute hand points straight down to the 6, it has travelled half of the way round, and we say the time is half past the hour. Why do we say half? Because there are 60 minutes in one hour, and half of 60 is 30. So 'half past' means 30 minutes past the hour. When the minute hand is on the 6, exactly 30 minutes have gone by since the last o'clock. If the hour hand is just past…
concepts.example: There is one clever thing to notice about the hour hand at half past. At half past a time, the hour hand is not pointing exactly at a number any more – it has crept halfway between two numbers. At half past 4, the hour…

concepts.title: Counting Minutes in Fives
concepts.explanation: To read all the other times, we need to know how to count the minutes. Here is the big secret of the clock: the numbers 1 to 12 are not only for the hours. They also help us count minutes. Each number on the clock is worth 5 minutes when we are reading the minute hand. This is because there are 60 minutes in an hour and 12 numbers on the clock, and 60 shared into 12 equal jumps makes 5 minutes for each jump. So we count the minutes by counting in fives as the minute hand moves from the 12. When the minute hand…
concepts.example: Minute hand on 12 = 0 minutes (an o'clock time)

concepts.title: Quarter Past and Quarter To
concepts.explanation: Some times are so common that they have special names, just as the number twelve has the special name 'a dozen'. Two of these special times are quarter past and quarter to. To understand them, picture a round mango sliced into four equal pieces. Each piece is called a quarter. In the same way, we can think of the clock face as being split into four quarters. Quarter past means the minute hand has moved one quarter of the way around the clock. One quarter of the way brings the minute hand to the 3. Counting in…
concepts.example: Quarter to means the minute hand has moved three quarters of the way around, and now there is only one quarter left before the next hour. Three quarters of the way brings the minute hand to the 9. Counting in fives from…

explorations.hint: Represent the information, name the rule, then solve one step at a time.

methods.title: How to read an analog clock
methods.example: The hour hand points to the 5 and the minute hand points to the 12. What time is it?
steps: Find the hour hand (short and thick). See which number it is on or just past. That is the hour.
Find the minute hand (long and thin). Count in fives from the 12 to where it points.
Say the time: the hour, then the minutes.
Check the special positions: 12, 3, 6 or 9 give o'clock, quarter past, half past or quarter to.

methods.title: How to write a time on a digital clock
methods.example: The minute hand points to the 6, and the hour hand sits between the 9 and the 10. What time is it?
steps: Write the hour first.
Write a dot ( : ) after the hour.
Write the minutes as two digits. Use a zero first if the minutes are less than ten (like 3:05).

methods.title: How to tell the part of the day
methods.example: The minute hand points to the 3, and the hour hand is just past the 2. What time is it?
steps: Look at what is happening – the activity is your clue.
Breakfast, waking, going to school = morning.
Lunch, playing in the sun = afternoon.
Dinner, bedtime, sunset = evening.

methods.title: Read a quarter to time
methods.example: The minute hand points to the 9, and the hour hand sits between the 7 and the 8. What time is it?
steps: Solution: The minute hand on the 9 means quarter to – there are 15 minutes left until the next hour.
The hour hand is between 7 and 8, so the next hour coming is 8.
The time is quarter to 8, written 7:45.
Remember: we name the hour that is coming.

methods.title: Count minutes in fives
methods.example: The minute hand points to the 7, and the hour hand is just past the 4. What time is it?
steps: Solution: Count in fives from the 12 to the 7: 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35.
So it is 35 minutes past the hour.
The hour is 4.
The time is 35 minutes past 4, written 4:35.

methods.title: Match analog to digital
methods.example: Halima's clock shows the minute hand on the 6 and the hour hand between the 3 and the 4. Write this time on a digital clock.
steps: Solution: Minute hand on 6 is half past (30 minutes).
Hour hand between 3 and 4 means the hour is 3.
So it is half past 3, which on a digital clock is 3:30.

workedExamples.title: Read an o'clock time
workedExamples.prompt: The hour hand points to the 5 and the minute hand points to the 12. What time is it?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The minute hand is on the 12, so this is an o'clock time – there are no extra minutes. The hour hand points to the 5. So the time is 5 o'clock, written 5:00.

workedExamples.title: Read a half past time
workedExamples.prompt: The minute hand points to the 6, and the hour hand sits between the 9 and the 10. What time is it?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The minute hand on the 6 means half past – 30 minutes past the hour. The hour hand is between 9 and 10, so we read the smaller number, 9. The time is half past 9, written 9:30.

workedExamples.title: Read a quarter past time
workedExamples.prompt: The minute hand points to the 3, and the hour hand is just past the 2. What time is it?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The minute hand on the 3 means quarter past – 15 minutes past the hour. The hour hand is just past the 2, so the hour is 2. The time is quarter past 2, written 2:15.

workedExamples.title: Read a quarter to time
workedExamples.prompt: The minute hand points to the 9, and the hour hand sits between the 7 and the 8. What time is it?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The minute hand on the 9 means quarter to – there are 15 minutes left until the next hour. The hour hand is between 7 and 8, so the next hour coming is 8. The time is quarter to 8, written 7:45. Remember: we name the hour that is coming.

workedExamples.title: Count minutes in fives
workedExamples.prompt: The minute hand points to the 7, and the hour hand is just past the 4. What time is it?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Count in fives from the 12 to the 7: 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35. So it is 35 minutes past the hour. The hour is 4. The time is 35 minutes past 4, written 4:35.

workedExamples.title: Match analog to digital
workedExamples.prompt: Halima's clock shows the minute hand on the 6 and the hour hand between the 3 and the 4. Write this time on a digital clock.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Minute hand on 6 is half past (30 minutes). Hour hand between 3 and 4 means the hour is 3. So it is half past 3, which on a digital clock is 3:30.

workedExamples.title: Morning, afternoon or evening
workedExamples.prompt: Yusuf eats his breakfast at 7 o'clock. Is this the morning, afternoon or evening 7 o'clock?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Breakfast is the first meal of the day, eaten early. The clue 'breakfast' tells us it is morning. So it is 7 o'clock in the morning.

workedExamples.title: A 'how long' problem
workedExamples.prompt: The Asr prayer is at 4 o'clock. It is now half past 3. How long must Omar wait?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: From half past 3 to 4 o'clock, the minute hand travels from the 6 up to the 12 – that is half of the clock, which is 30 minutes. So Omar waits 30 minutes, which is half an hour.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 9
workedExamples.prompt: Count in fives to find the minutes: a) minute hand on the 4 b) minute hand on the 7 c) minute hand on the 9.
workedExamples.solution: a) 20 minutes, b) 35 minutes, c) 45 minutes.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 10
workedExamples.prompt: Match each analog time to its digital time: half past 8, quarter past 1, 12 o'clock, quarter to 3. (Digital: 1:15, 2:45, 8:30, 12:00.)
workedExamples.solution: half past 8 – 8:30; quarter past 1 – 1:15; 12 o'clock – 12:00; quarter to 3 – 2:45.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 11
workedExamples.prompt: The minute hand is on the 9 and the hour hand is between the 6 and the 7. What time is it? Remember to name the hour that is coming.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 12
workedExamples.prompt: Explain in your own words why 'quarter to 4' is written 3:45 on a digital clock.
workedExamples.solution: 1) The digital clock shows the hour we are still in – the 3 o'clock hour – with 45 minutes gone, while 'quarter to 4' looks ahead to the coming hour. Both name the same moment.

activities.title: Make Your Own Paper Clock
activities.materials: a paper plate or a circle of card, a pencil, and two strips of card for the hands, and a paper fastener or a blob of clay.
steps: Write the numbers 1 to 12 around the edge of the plate, with the 12 at the top, the 6 at the bottom, the 3 on the right and the 9 on the left.
Cut one short, thick strip for the hour hand and one long, thin strip for the minute hand.
Fix both hands to the centre so they can turn.
Set your clock to o'clock, half past, quarter past and quarter to times, and say each one out loud.
Keep your paper clock – you can use it all through this unit to practise any time you like.

activities.title: Clock Scavenger Hunt
activities.materials: just your eyes and a notebook.
steps: Walk around your home and find as many clocks as you can – on the wall, on a phone, on the oven, on a watch.
For each clock, write down whether it is analog or digital.
Read the time on each clock and write it down.
Which kind of clock did you find more of? Talk about it with your family.

activities.title: Beat the Clock (Counting in Fives Game)
activities.materials: your paper clock from Activity 1, or any clock.
steps: One player moves the minute hand to a number and keeps the hour hand still.
The other player counts in fives from the 12 to find the minutes as fast as they can.
Swap roles after each turn. Give a point for every correct count.
Play until someone reaches ten points. This makes counting in fives quick and automatic.
This game trains the most important clock skill of all – counting the minutes in fives.

activities.title: My Day Timeline (Investigation)
steps: Draw a long line across your paper. Mark the morning at one end, the afternoon in the middle, and the evening at the other end.
Think about your real day. Write the time you wake for Fajr, eat breakfast, go to school, eat lunch, play, eat dinner, and go to bed.
Place each activity on your timeline at about the right time.
Look at your timeline. Which activities happen in the morning, which in the afternoon, and which in the evening?

activities.title: Time Snap (Family Game)
activities.materials: two sets of cards – one set with times in words (like 'half past 6') and one set with digital times (like '6:30').
steps: Make about eight pairs, each pair being the same time in words and in digital form.
Shuffle all the cards and lay them face down in a grid.
Take turns to turn over two cards. If they show the same time (one in words, one digital), you keep the pair.
The player with the most pairs at the end wins. Say each time out loud as you turn the cards.

activities.title: Prayer-Time Clock Challenge
activities.materials: your paper clock and the times your family prays.
steps: Ask a grown-up to tell you roughly what time each of the five daily prayers is.
Set your paper clock to show one of the prayer times.
Say the time in words and in digital form, and say whether it is morning, afternoon or evening.
Do this for each prayer time. Which prayers are in the morning? Which are in the evening?

After your activities, talk or write about: Which activity helped you understand the clock best? What was the trickiest kind of time to read – quarter past, quarter to, or something else? What would you like more practice with? Share your answer with your teacher at your next session.

rules.title: The Two Hands Rule
rules.text: Short, thick hand = HOUR hand. It moves slowly. Long, thin hand = MINUTE hand. It goes all the way round in one hour. Read the hour hand first, then the minute hand.

rules.title: The Special Positions Rule
rules.text: Minute hand on 12 = o'clock (0 minutes). Minute hand on 3 = quarter past (15 minutes). Minute hand on 6 = half past (30 minutes). Minute hand on 9 = quarter to (15 minutes before the next hour).

rules.title: The Counting in Fives Rule
rules.text: Each clock number is worth 5 minutes for the minute hand. Count from the 12: 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35, 40, 45, 50, 55, 60.

terms: A clock that shows numbers, like 7:30
The minute hand is on 12; a whole hour
15 minutes before the next hour; minute hand on 9
commonMistakes: Name the hour that is coming

realProblems.prompt: During Ramadan in Mombasa, the family eats their pre-dawn meal at 4 o'clock and breaks their fast at 7 o'clock in the evening. How many hours pass between these two meals? (Hint: count the hours from 4 in the morning to 7 in the evening.)
realProblems.answer: 1) 15 hours (from 4 in the morning to 7 in the evening).
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 1) 15 hours (from 4 in the morning to 7 in the evening).

realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 1) The digital clock shows the hour we are still in – the 3 o'clock hour – with 45 minutes gone, while 'quarter to 4' looks ahead to the coming hour. Both name the same moment.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain in your own words why 'quarter to 4' is written 3:45 on a digital clock.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 1) The digital clock shows the hour we are still in – the 3 o'clock hour – with 45 minutes gone, while 'quarter to 4' looks ahead to the coming hour. Both name the same moment.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Idris looks at a clock. The minute hand is on the 5 and the hour hand is just past the 2. Count in fives to find the exact time.
Sahra says, 'At half past 6, the hour hand points straight at the 6.' Is she right? Explain what the hour hand really does at half past.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: She is not quite right. At half past 6 the hour hand sits halfway between the 6 and the 7, not straight on the 6, because half the hour has already passed.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Put these times in order from earliest in the day to latest: 7 o'clock in the morning, half past 12, quarter to 6 in the evening, 9 o'clock in the morning.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 7 o'clock in the morning, 9 o'clock in the morning, half past 12, quarter to 6 in the evening.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain the key idea in Counting Minutes in Fives.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: To read all the other times, we need to know how to count the minutes. Here is the big secret of the clock: the numbers 1 to 12 are not only for the hours. They also help us count minutes. Each number on the clock is worth 5 minutes when we are reading the minute hand. This is because there are 60 minutes in an hour and 12 numbers on the clock, and 60 shared into 12 equal jumps makes 5 minutes for each jump. So we count the minutes by counting in fives as the minute hand moves from the 12. When the minute hand…

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain the key idea in Quarter Past and Quarter To.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Some times are so common that they have special names, just as the number twelve has the special name 'a dozen'. Two of these special times are quarter past and quarter to. To understand them, picture a round mango sliced into four equal pieces. Each piece is called a quarter. In the same way, we can think of the clock face as being split into four quarters. Quarter past means the minute hand has moved one quarter of the way around the clock. One quarter of the way brings the minute hand to the 3. Counting in…

selfAssessment: I can this unit is designed for self-paced learning. That means you move at your own speed. Read each explanation slowly, look carefully at the clock pictures described in words, and practise on a real clock whenever you can. Your AI tutor is available day and night to give you extra practice, to describe a clock face in a new way, or to check your answers. Your teacher will also meet with you about twice a week to help with anything that feels tricky.

I can why does telling the time matter so much? Time helps us organise our day and know when to do things. Just as we use a ruler to measure length and a scale to measure weight, we use a clock to measure time. Traders know when the market opens. Families know when to gather for prayer, because the five daily prayers each come at their own time. Bus drivers between Mogadishu and Kismaayo follow a timetable. When you become confident at telling the time, you gain a skill you will use for the rest of your life – and you become more independent, because you can plan and organise yourself.

I can as you work, it will help enormously to keep a real clock beside you – a wall clock, a watch, or a toy clock you can turn with your hands. Mathematics is not a subject you can learn only by reading; you must do it. Move the hands, say the time out loud, and picture the clock in your mind. If you read a time wrongly, that is not a failure; it simply shows exactly what to practise next. Every person who reads a clock easily once had to learn these very same ideas, one careful step at a time.
I can name the two main kinds of clock: the analog clock and the digital clock.
I can point to the parts of an analog clock: the face, the numbers, the hour hand and the minute hand.
I can read times to the o'clock (for example 3 o'clock).
I can read times to the half past (for example half past 4).
I can read times to the quarter past and quarter to the hour.
